key_insight: A combined tension–bending–bearing load is identifiable from a crack-front stress-intensity profile only when the three elementary load profiles are sufficiently linearly independent along the crack front.
engineering_relevance: This gives fracture engineers a principled test for when load reconstruction from SIF data is reliable, and when an uncertainty-aware inverse estimate is the safer output.
method: Built a linear forward operator mapping load magnitudes to the crack-front SIF profile.
Analyzed identifiability via linear dependence of the three elementary load profiles and an intrinsic stability margin.
Used a simplex-constrained, set-valued inverse estimator that returns calibrated uncertainty in ill-posed regimes.
evidence_signal: On the SIFBench corner-crack finite-element data, the typical geometry was well posed while a substantial minority was genuinely ill posed, with validation performed under controlled synthetic noise.
watch_item: The approach depends on known geometry and synthetic-noise evaluation, so real-world performance may degrade if geometry, boundary conditions, or noise characteristics differ.
paper_title: When is the combined load identifiable from a stress-intensity profile? A coupled forward-inverse study on SIFBench finite-element data
